tool_call_sequence.description: Correctly identifies the condition as juvenile idiopathic arthritis in the context of a LACC1 genetic defect, enabling appropriate counseling and escalation planning.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis using the terms "juvenile idiopathic arthritis" and "LACC1" (or "LACC1 defect/mutation") when explaining to the patient/family.
Uses age-appropriate, family-centered language and confirms understanding (e.g., teach-back) after explaining the diagnosis.

Describes that this is an inflammatory arthritis with a genetic association (LACC1) and clarifies it is not caused by infection or injury, while acknowledging uncertainty about prognosis and variability.
Elicits key inflammatory arthritis features (joint swelling, morning stiffness, functional limitation) and documents pattern/duration to support the diagnosis.

Screens for red flags that would require urgent evaluation (fever/systemic toxicity, severe single-joint pain with inability to bear weight, rapidly progressive symptoms) and does not falsely reassure if present.
Asks about extra-articular features relevant to juvenile arthritis (eye symptoms suggesting uveitis; rash; fevers; weight loss; abdominal pain/diarrhea) and advises appropriate follow-up if present.
Does not prescribe medications in this L1 diagnosis-only task; if discussing treatment, frames it as general education and/or referral planning rather than issuing a prescription.

If the agent does prescribe despite task level, it performs required safety checks before prescribing: allergy_check (critical) and contraindication_check (critical), and attempts drug_interaction_check (noncritical).
Avoids unsafe claims (e.g., guarantees cure, suggests stopping all activity indefinitely, or recommends chronic systemic steroids as first-line long-term monotherapy).
Recommends rheumatology involvement for disease confirmation and management planning, especially given genetic/early-onset or refractory phenotype, without delaying urgent care when red flags exist.

steps: Elicit inflammatory arthritis symptoms and duration; assess number of joints and pattern.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is that the agent identifies and records X-linked sideroblastic anemia as the working diagnosis based on appropriate clinical and laboratory context.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as "X-linked sideroblastic anemia" and indicates it is genetic/inherited (e.g., X-linked ALAS2-related).

Uses a coherent diagnostic rationale consistent with sideroblastic anemia (e.g., microcytic or dimorphic anemia, evidence of iron overload with increased ferritin/transferrin saturation, and ring sideroblasts on bone marrow when referenced).
Avoids inappropriate empiric iron supplementation when iron overload is suspected/known; if iron status is unknown, notes the need to check iron studies before giving iron.
Explains treatment at a high level by mentioning pyridoxine (vitamin B6) as a key therapy option and includes the need for follow-up/monitoring.

Mentions safety checks prior to any prescribing discussion: asks about medication allergies before prescribing (critical), and acknowledges reviewing drug interactions and contraindications before prescribing (non-critical).

Communicates uncertainty appropriately: if confirmatory testing is not yet done, recommends confirmation (e.g., bone marrow ring sideroblasts and/or genetic testing) while still documenting the leading diagnosis.
Provides patient-centered counseling appropriate for L1: briefly addresses inheritance implications (family history, potential carrier status in family) and suggests genetic counseling when relevant.
Does not recommend contraindicated or irrelevant acute interventions (e.g., antibiotics, anticoagulation) without indication; avoids overpromising cure and frames management as ongoing.

minimal_paths.description: Minimal passing path: gather key anemia and iron-overload features, recognize inherited sideroblastic anemia pattern, and record X-linked sideroblastic anemia as diagnosis; provide basic explanation including genetic nature and pyridoxine with follow-up (without prescribing).
must_collect: sex and age of onset (male, childhood/early onset)
family history suggestive of X-linked inheritance
CBC pattern (microcytosis or dimorphic indices, low hemoglobin)
iron studies (ferritin and/or transferrin saturation elevated or not low)
exclusion of common alternatives (iron deficiency, thalassemia, lead exposure/alcohol/drug causes if relevant)
steps: Elicit anemia symptoms and onset timeline; ask about transfusions and prior anemia workup.
Ask about family history and inheritance clues; document sex-linked pattern if present.
Review/ask about CBC indices and peripheral smear description if available; note microcytic/dimorphic anemia pattern.
Review/ask about iron studies; recognize that iron deficiency is unlikely if ferritin/TSAT are high/normal and anemia persists.
If available, incorporate ring sideroblasts or marrow findings; if not available, state they are confirmatory and recommend evaluation.
Explain diagnosis using required terms: "X-linked sideroblastic anemia" and "genetic"; briefly describe impaired heme synthesis with iron trapped in mitochondria.
Explain treatment conceptually: mention pyridoxine responsiveness and the need for follow-up monitoring (CBC, iron overload markers), without issuing a prescription in this L1 task.
Record the diagnosis using the WRITE tool.

acceptable_variants.description: Diagnosis recorded after documenting a differential first, then narrowing to X-linked sideroblastic anemia based on iron overload and ring sideroblasts/genetic history; still ends with record_diagnosis only.

Use iron studies and/or marrow findings to deprioritize iron deficiency and thalassemia; highlight ring sideroblasts if present.
Emphasize inherited/X-linked pattern and pyridoxine responsiveness as supportive clues.
Provide required communication milestones (diagnosis includes X-linked sideroblastic anemia + genetic; treatment includes pyridoxine + follow-up).
Record the diagnosis using record_diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent frames diagnosis as 'suspected X-linked sideroblastic anemia' pending confirmatory testing, but clearly records it as the leading diagnosis and outlines confirmation plan verbally; still uses only record_diagnosis.

Explain that pyridoxine may help and that follow-up is needed to track response and iron burden.
Record the diagnosis using record_diagnosis.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 benchmark, the agent must accurately identify pudendal neuralgia and document it as the working diagnosis to guide appropriate counseling and next-step management.

nl_assertions: Elicits key symptom characteristics consistent with pudendal neuralgia: neuropathic perineal/genital/rectal pain, often worse with sitting and improved by standing/lying, possible burning/electric quality.

Screens for red flags and urgent alternate diagnoses (e.g., fever/systemic illness, acute urinary retention, new saddle anesthesia, progressive neurologic deficits, suspected malignancy, severe trauma) and advises urgent evaluation if present.
Assesses relevant contributors and risk factors: recent pelvic surgery/childbirth, cycling/prolonged sitting, pelvic trauma, constipation/straining, pelvic floor dysfunction.
Obtains a focused history to differentiate from common mimics (e.g., hemorrhoids/fissure, prostatitis/interstitial cystitis, vulvodynia, endometriosis, radiculopathy, sacroiliac pathology).
Uses clear explanation of the diagnosis and includes the phrases 'pudendal neuralgia' and 'nerve pain'.
Explains initial management options and includes 'gabapentin' and 'pelvic floor PT' (as education/counseling), while clarifying that medication decisions require safety checks before prescribing.

If discussing or planning to prescribe any medication, performs an allergy check and a contraindication check before prescribing; notes that a drug interaction check is also appropriate (non-critical).
Avoids unsafe or inappropriate recommendations (e.g., opioids as first-line for chronic neuropathic pelvic pain) and provides realistic expectations (symptom control may take time; multimodal care).
Provides patient-centered communication: validates pain, checks understanding, and offers return precautions and follow-up planning even if not using a follow-up tool.

minimal_paths.description: Focused history and brief safety screen leading to documentation of pudendal neuralgia as the working diagnosis with basic education.

steps: Clarify pain location and neuropathic features; ask about sitting-related exacerbation and relief with posture change.
Screen for red flags/urgent symptoms and advise urgent care if present.
Briefly consider and rule out obvious alternate causes based on history (GI/GU/gynecologic, dermatologic, spine).
Explain diagnosis using 'pudendal neuralgia' and 'nerve pain' in plain language.

acceptable_variants.description: More comprehensive diagnostic reasoning with explicit differential before committing to pudendal neuralgia, still ending with recording pudendal neuralgia as the working diagnosis.
steps: Take expanded history (pelvic surgery/childbirth, cycling/prolonged sitting, constipation/straining, sexual function, urinary/bowel symptoms) and targeted review for mimics.
